subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Player Components
- **Player boards** — 1 per player; hexagonal grid for building a 3D landscape.
- **Board spaces** — Each board has multiple hex spaces where tokens can be stacked up to 3 high.

#### Tokens
- **Landscape tokens** — Colored wooden discs representing terrain types.
- **5 terrain colors** — Green (trees/forests), Yellow (fields/plains), Gray (mountains/rock), Brown (buildings/settlements), Blue (water/lakes).
- **Token quantity** — Large supply of each color for all players.

#### Cards
- **Animal cards** — Cards showing specific 3D token arrangements that score VP when completed.
- **Animal types** — Various animals (deer, eagle, fish, butterfly, fox, etc.) each requiring unique patterns.
- **Landscape bonus cards** — Optional bonus scoring for terrain arrangements.

#### Central Pool
- **Token display** — Central area where tokens are drawn from.
- **Refill system** — Tokens drawn from a bag to replenish the central display.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
1. **Distribute boards** — Give each player 1 personal hex board.
2. **Fill central pool** — Draw tokens from the bag to fill the central display.
3. **Deal animal cards** — Deal a selection of animal cards face up; players draft or a shared pool is available.
4. **Landscape bonus cards** — Optionally include landscape bonus scoring cards.
5. **Token supply** — Place bag of tokens centrally.
6. **Scoring reference** — Make animal card scoring patterns visible to all players.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Each Player Begins With
- **Empty board** — All hex spaces are empty; no tokens placed.
- **Animal cards** — Each player selects animal cards they will attempt to complete.
- **No tokens in hand** — Tokens are drafted from the central pool each turn.

#### Animal Card Selection
- **Draft or deal** — Depending on variant, players draft animal cards or receive a random selection.
- **Scoring targets** — Each animal card shows a specific 3D arrangement to build on your board.
- **Difficulty varies** — Some animals require complex multi-level patterns; others are simpler.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
1. **Central pool filled** — Ensure the token display has the correct number of tokens.
2. **First player chosen** — Random selection or youngest player.
3. **Clockwise play** — Turns proceed clockwise.
4. **Draft tokens** — On your turn, take tokens from the central pool and place them on your board.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### On Your Turn
1. **Draft tokens** — Take exactly 3 tokens from a single group in the central display.
2. **Place tokens** — Place all 3 tokens on your personal board, one at a time.
3. **Stacking rules** — Tokens can be placed on empty hex spaces (level 1), on top of existing tokens (level 2), or on top of a level-2 stack (level 3, maximum height).
4. **Check completions** — If any animal card patterns are fully matched on your board, score that card.
5. **Refill display** — The central display is refilled for the next player.

#### Placement Constraints
- **Maximum height 3** — No stack can exceed 3 tokens high.
- **Support required** — A token at level 2+ must rest on a token below it.
- **Color matters** — The top-visible color of each stack determines what patterns are present when viewed from above.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Token Placement Strategy
- **Ground level (level 1)** — Place directly on empty hex spaces; forms the base of your landscape.
- **Level 2** — Stack on top of a level-1 token; the top color is what counts for pattern matching.
- **Level 3** — Stack on top of level 2; creates mountains, tall trees, or elevated terrain.
- **Color coverage** — The visible color from above determines which animal patterns you are building.

#### Animal Card Patterns
- **Top-down view** — Animal cards show a pattern of colors as seen from above your board.
- **3D arrangements** — Some animals require specific heights (e.g., a mountain animal needs gray tokens at level 3).
- **Adjacency** — Pattern tiles must be adjacent to each other on your hex grid.
- **Completing a card** — When all required tokens are in the correct positions and heights, claim the animal card for VP.

#### Token Colors & Terrain
- **Green (Trees)** — Often required at various heights for forest animals.
- **Yellow (Fields)** — Low-level terrain for prairie animals.
- **Gray (Mountains)** — Typically stacked high for mountain-dwelling animals.
- **Brown (Buildings)** — Settlements and structures.
- **Blue (Water)** — Usually at ground level for aquatic animals.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### 3D Landscape Building
- **Height matters** — Unlike flat tile-placement games, Harmonies uses vertical stacking as a core mechanic.
- **Layered colors** — A space can show different colors at different heights; only the topmost color counts for pattern matching.
- **Covering tokens** — Placing a token on top of another changes the visible landscape, potentially completing or breaking patterns.

#### Animal Card Variety
- **Simple animals** — Require 2-3 tokens in basic adjacent patterns (e.g., 2 adjacent green at level 1).
- **Complex animals** — Require multi-height arrangements across 4-5 hexes.
- **Point scaling** — More complex patterns are worth more VP.
- **Multiple completions** — You can complete the same animal pattern in different areas of your board if you have multiple copies of that card.

#### Landscape Bonuses
- **Terrain coverage** — Bonus points for covering specific portions of your board with certain terrain types.
- **Height bonuses** — Some bonuses reward reaching level 3 in multiple locations.

#### Central Pool Dynamics
- **Group drafting** — Tokens are arranged in groups; you must take all 3 from one group.
- **Color selection** — The available colors in each group constrain your options.
- **Reading the pool** — Anticipate which groups opponents will take to plan your drafts.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### End Trigger
- **Token supply exhausted** — The game ends when the central pool cannot be fully refilled.
- **Final turn** — All players complete an equal number of turns.

#### Scoring
- **Animal cards** — Score VP printed on each completed animal card.
- **Landscape bonus** — Score any landscape bonus cards if conditions are met.
- **Incomplete patterns** — Animal cards not completed score 0 VP.

#### Winner
- **Most VP wins** — Sum completed animal card points + landscape bonuses.
- **Tiebreaker** — Player with more completed animal cards wins; if still tied, shared victory.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
- **Select complementary animals** — Choose animal cards that share similar color/height requirements to minimize conflicts.
- **Build a foundation** — Place ground-level tokens strategically to support future stacking.
- **Plan heights early** — Identify where you need level 2 and level 3 stacks and start building those zones.
- **Draft with purpose** — Take token groups that serve multiple animal patterns simultaneously.
- **Avoid overcommitting** — Do not lock into one pattern early; keep options open for what the pool offers.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
- **Ignoring height requirements** — Many animals require specific stack heights; placing flat boards misses them.
- **Covering needed colors** — Stacking a token on top of a color you need visible breaks existing patterns.
- **Taking tokens without a plan** — Drafting 3 tokens you cannot usefully place wastes a turn.
- **Choosing conflicting animals** — Animal cards that require different colors in the same area are impossible to complete simultaneously.
- **Neglecting landscape bonuses** — These can contribute significant points and should be planned alongside animals.
- **Spreading too thin** — Trying to complete too many animal cards leads to none being finished.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
- **Which group to draft** — Evaluate which 3-token group best serves your current and future patterns.
- **Stack or spread** — Whether to build height for complex animals or spread tokens for simpler ones.
- **Abandon vs commit** — When a partially built animal pattern becomes unlikely, decide whether to pivot or push through.
- **Covering trade-offs** — Stacking changes the visible landscape; weigh completing one pattern against breaking another.
- **Blocking opponents** — Whether to take a group your opponent needs, even if it is not your first choice.
